query: Shine-Dalgarno sequence role in prokaryote translation

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Protein Synthesis -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: Ribosomes:

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Structure -- Fundamentals -- rRNA
retrieved_docs.content: rRNA forms ribosomes, which are essential in protein synthesis. A ribosome contains a large and small ribosomal subunit. In prokaryotes, a small 30S and large 50S ribosomal subunit make up a 70S ribosome. In eukaryotes, the 40S and 60S subunit form an 80S ribosome. The ribosomes contain an exit (E), peptidyl (P), and acceptor (A) site to bind aminoacyl-tRNAs and link amino acids together to create polypeptides.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Polymerase -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: All RNAPs contain a core. This RNA-synthesizing core generates 5’ to 3’ RNA chains by hydrolyzing pyrophosphate from nucleoside triphosphates. Bacterial core RNAP is the simplest, comprised of five subunits: beta, beta prime, two alphas, and omega. Together, the large beta and beta prime subunits form a claw with the reactive magnesium ion. [2] In the center of the claw is a catalytically active site. The initiation of the core assembly occurs by the dimerization of the N-terminal domain of the alpha subunits, followed by beta and with omega subsequently serving as the chaperone for beta prime. A flexible linker tethers the C-terminal domains of alphas and serves important regulatory roles. Archaea and eukaryotic core subunits are also highly homologous to bacterial RNAP. Bacterial, archaeal, and eukaryotic RNAPs all resemble a crab claw with an enzyme active site located at the bottom cleft of the claw. [3] [4] Here, a catalytic metal magnesium ion as well as an absolutely-conserved motif of NADFDGD and three invariant residues are found. [3] [4] The architecture surrounding the cleft is also highly conserved among all three domains of life, suggesting that this mechanism of RNA synthesis gets conserved from bacteria to humans.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Structure -- Molecular Level -- How is it made?
retrieved_docs.content: Prokaryotic RNA undergoes Rho-dependent or Rho-independent termination. In Rho-dependent termination, a Rho factor helicase binds to C-rich sites on the RNA, and ATP hydrolysis powers Rho to unwind the DNA-RNA complex and release the RNA transcript. [11] On the other hand, Rho-independent termination utilizes a hairpin loop that causes the RNA polymerase to stall and allows the RNA transcript to be released.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Structure -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: The mechanism of RNA translation into protein is carried out in three steps: initiation, elongation, and termination. During initiation, a tRNA and ribosome join an mRNA. The start codon, AUG, codes for methionine and will always be the first amino acid in the sequence. After initiation, the ribosome moves along the mRNA in the 5’-3’ direction, reading the codons and binding tRNAs carrying the respective amino acids for which the codons are coding. The sequence of amino acids progressively becomes longer and terminates upon reaching a stop codon. The stop codons are UAG, UAA, and UGA. The polypeptide is then released from the ribosomal complex and modified into an active protein. In eukaryotic cells, translation occurs after transcription, while in prokaryotic cells, transcription and translation occur simultaneously.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Protein Synthesis -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Briefly, translation can be broken down into three phases initiation, elongation, and termination. Initiation consists of identifying the exact site in the sequence of nucleotides in an mRNA to begin translation. This process has significant differences between eukaryotes (described here) and prokaryotes. Upon identification of the start site for translation, elongation ensues as the ribosome moves along the mRNA “reading” groups of three nucleotides that specify each amino acid added to the growing polypeptide chain. Finally, termination occurs when the ribosome encounters one of three termination codons, and the completed protein gets released from the ribosome.

retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial DNA Mutations -- Clinical Significance -- Protein Synthesis Inhibitor
retrieved_docs.content: Antibiotic resistance mechanisms can also occur by incorporating resistance genes into plasmids, transposons, and integrons. These genes spread through horizontal transfer by conjugation, transformation, or transduction mechanisms. However, the mutation is essential for the evolution or assortment of these genes.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Primary Protein Structure -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: The processes of transcription and translation are similar and different in eukaryotes and prokaryotes. This section only focuses on eukaryotic molecular mechanisms.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Protein Synthesis -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: The eukaryotic ribosome has two subunits: a 40S small subunit and a 60S large subunit. Together, the eukaryotic ribosome is 80S. There are several sites of functional significance, but the most important ones are the A (aminoacyl), P (peptidyl), and E (exit) sites. The eukaryotic ribosome is a ribonucleoprotein complex composed of 4 RNAs and 80 proteins. Many of the functions of the ribosome, including catalyzing peptide bond formation, are attributed to ribosomal RNA (rRNA) rather than ribosomal proteins, which instead play a primary role in subunit assembly. Ribosomes can be found either adherent to membranes of the endoplasmic reticulum or free within the cytoplasm. [1] Bacterial ribosomes have two subunits, 30S and 50S, that join to form a 70S particle. In general, bacterial ribosomes are smaller than their eukaryotic counterparts, including fewer ribosomal proteins (55) and shorter rRNAs (3 in total). Certain regions of rRNA and some of the ribosomal proteins remain conserved between bacteria and eukaryotes. Other regions of rRNA and proteins are unique to either eukaryotes or bacteria and account in part for differences in mechanisms of protein synthesis discussed above. Eukaryotic cells contain a second type of ribosome found within the mitochondrion, which maintains a system of protein synthesis distinct from that found in the cytoplasm. Despite their presence in eukaryotic cells, the origins of the mitochondrial ribosome are traceable to bacteria, consistent with the endosymbiont theory of mitochondrial origins.  Care must be taken during antibiotic development to avoid targeting characteristics of the mitochondrial ribosome shared with bacterial ribosomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Proteins -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Translation

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Primary Protein Structure -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: After a mature mRNA transcript leaves the nucleus, a ribosome attaches to the transcript to begin translation. The initiation site in both prokaryotes and eukaryotes is not composed of a unique initiation sequence but is more probably a multisubstrate enzyme system based on substrate specificity. [5] [6] [7] [8] A postulated mechanism (scanning mechanism) predicts that the ribosomes initiate protein synthesis by recognizing and binding the 5′ tail of the mRNA corresponding to a 7-methylguanosine referred to as "cap." [9] The first methionine codon located in the 5′ ends of the mRNA is the initiator codon. Several factors bind to this initiation sequence (IF4F initiation factors), forming the ribosome-eIF2-GTP-Met-tRNA preinitiation complex (PIC). [10] A favorable initiation sequence in mammals is known as “Kozak consensus”: 5’ (A/G)CCAUGG 3’. The PIC scans the mRNA in the 5’ region until it reaches the AUG start codon complementary to Met-tRNA's anticodon. The first AUG codon (“leaky scanning”) can be skipped to use a downstream AUG. This creates protein isoforms when the downstream AUG is located in the same open reading frame as the first 1 or a completely different protein if it is not in-frame.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Primary Protein Structure -- Mechanism -- Translation
retrieved_docs.content: Elongation occurs as the ribosome reads in the mRNA strand 5’-3’ direction through an open reading frame (ORF). The ORF is a continuous stretch of codons that begins with the AUG start codon and ends at 1 of 3 termination codons (UAG, UAA, or UGA), not associated with any amino acid. The ribosome moves 1 triplet at a time along the mRNA. An aminoacyl-tRNA, complexed with eukaryotic elongation factor 1 (eEF1), hydrolyzes GTP to release eEF1. Thus, it provides energy for the aminoacyl-tRNA (with the appropriate anticodon to match the mRNA codon) to bind to the A site. Elongation of the polypeptide occurs through the stepwise addition of amino acids, bound by peptide bonds, between the amino acids (attached to the tRNA), bound to the A site and P site. The tRNA molecule bound on the P site is called the peptidyl-tRNA since it bears the polypeptide chain. For clarification, the aminoacyl-tRNA only holds a new single amino acid (at the A site) to be added to the growing polypeptide chain. Translocation of tRNA molecules from the A site to the P site occurs through GTP hydrolysis catalyzed by eukaryotic elongation factor 2 (eEF2). As this shift moves the tRNA from the A site to the P site, the tRNA already on the P site moves to the E site, now called the unloaded tRNA. From here, the unloaded tRNA is released from the E site. Termination of translation happens when eukaryotic translation termination factor 1 (eRF1) recognizes 1 of the stop codons in the mRNA transcript. [17] Eukaryotic translation termination factor 3 (eRF1) is recruited to hydrolyze (using GTP) the polypeptide chain from the tRNA occupying the P site. [17] The newly formed polypeptide chain is released from the ribosome, and the ribosomal subunits dissociate.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Polymerase -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: RNAP’s mechanism of transcribing DNA into RNA is highly conserved across prokaryotes, archaea, and eukaryotes. To begin the process of bacterial transcription, along with core polymerase, an additional specificity factor is also necessary. These specificity proteins recognize certain elements in the promoter DNA and determine the start site of transcription. In bacteria, these specificity factors have the name of sigma subunits. Sigma, together with core, form the bacterial RNAP. Though research has now identified hundreds of sigma, the main sigma factor, sigma70 in Escherichia coli or sigma A in other bacteria, is responsible for exponential growth and regulation of housekeeping genes. [20] Other alternate sigma factors are used during times of stress and/or different growth conditions. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Protein Synthesis -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: Protein synthesis involves a complex interplay of many macromolecules.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Inducible Operon -- Cellular
retrieved_docs.content: The lac operon spans over 5300 base pairs and contains 3 structural genes: LacZ, LacY, and LacA. A single lac promoter coordinates its transcription between base pairs -36 and -7 from the transcription start site. The DNA-dependent RNA-polymerase binds to the promoter during each transcription cycle and initiates transcription. This produces a single polycistronic mRNA containing multiple independent Stop and Start codons. Each of the 3 proteins is translated separately and not cleaved from a single polypeptide precursor. [1] [6] [9] Structural genes of the lac operon encode proteins that coordinately metabolize lactose .

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Polymerase -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Prevention of gene expression is one way in which antibiotics can kill bacteria. Since transcription is an essential process for all organisms, the transcription machinery is an extremely attractive target for the development of new antibiotics. Rifampicin is a commonly used antibiotic against Mycobacterium tuberculosis . Belonging to the ansamycin class of antibiotics, rifampicin binds to the beta subunit of RNAP within the DNA/RNA channel and prevents the formation of the second or third phosphodiester bond, inducing the release of short abortives and subsequently blocking nascent RNA extension. [29] [30] [31] Interestingly, bacteria have quickly evolved to develop resistance to rifampicin. Mutations within the beta subunit of RNAP (S531L, H526Y, and D516V) account for about for 41, 36, and 9% of resistant tuberculosis strains, respectively. [31] Other antibiotics that block nascent RNA extension include sorangicin and GE23077. [32] Another commonly used antibiotic, fidaxomicin, also binds to Clostridium difficile RNAP. Instead of the beta subunit, fidaxomicin binds to the switch region of RNAP. [33] This binding prevents RPo formation. The proposed mechanism is that fidaxomicin prevents the correct spatial orientation of Region 2 and Region 4 for recognition of the -10 and -35 core promoter elements, respectively. [33] [34] Other antibiotics that also target the RNAP switch region include squaramides, myxopyronin, corallopyronin, and ripostatin. [32] Though only very few antibiotics have reached the clinical market, there are also other many antibiotics which inhibit transcription by varying processes. The SB-2 series disrupt holoenzyme assembly, pseudoridmycin is a nucleoside analog, and salinamides targets the mobile elements of the primary channel. [32]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2028
retrieved_docs.content: Deoxyribonukleasy, an enzyme that catalyzes the cleavage of DNA strands, encompasses within its broader class the more specific enzyme streptodornase; streptodornase represents a narrower subclass of deoxyribonukleasy with distinct functional properties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1383
retrieved_docs.content: Aminoglycoside antibacterials constitute a major class of antibiotics that act by binding to bacterial ribosomal RNA and disrupting protein synthesis. Within this class, streptomycin is recognized as a child or subclass member, exemplifying the typical aminoglycoside mechanism of action and spectrum of activity against aerobic gram‑negative organisms and certain mycobacteria. Although a triple incorrectly linked daptomycin to aminoglycoside antibacterials, the correct classification places daptomycin in a separate antibiotic family; it is a lipopeptide antibacterial that exerts its effect by inserting into bacterial cell membranes and causing rapid depolarization, leading to cell death. Understanding that streptomycin belongs to the aminoglycoside group while daptomycin belongs to a distinct lipopeptide class is essential for accurate antimicrobial selection and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1593
retrieved_docs.content: biosynthetic pathways related to protein and muscle growth. Finally, trisaccharides, as short carbohydrate chains, can serve as substrates or regulatory sugars that modulate glycosylation and other carbohydrate‑based biosynthetic processes. Together, these entities illustrate the wide range of chemical, pharmacologic, and biological factors that can define, regulate, or modify biosynthetic mechanisms, underscoring their importance for understanding metabolic regulation and for developing therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1378
retrieved_docs.content: Mupirocin is associated with increased protein synthesis, indicating that increased protein synthesis is a physiologic effect of mupirocin.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 397
retrieved_docs.content: Actinomycin is chemically related to depsipeptidy, indicating that it belongs to or is associated with the broader class of depsipeptide compounds. In addition, Actinomycin functions as a DNA minor groove binding agent, placing it within the category of agents that specifically bind to the minor groove of DNA to interfere with transcription and replication processes. This dual characterization highlights both its structural classification as a depsipeptide‑related molecule and its pharmacological role in targeting DNA architecture.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1412
retrieved_docs.content: Increased protein synthesis is a physiologic effect associated with both mupirocin and nusinersen. Mupirocin, an antimicrobial agent, and nusinersen, an antisense oligonucleotide therapy, each promote the up‑regulation of protein production as part of their pharmacologic actions, highlighting a shared mechanistic outcome despite their different therapeutic indications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1517
retrieved_docs.content: Micronomicin is classified as an aminoglycoside antibacterial, placing it within the aminoglycoside antibacterials class of antibiotics that inhibit bacterial protein synthesis by binding to the 30 S ribosomal subunit. This categorization identifies micronomicin as a member of a drug group commonly used to treat serious Gram‑negative infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1256
retrieved_docs.content: Inhibitory enzymů function as a parent category for inhibitory syntézy proteinů, meaning that enzymes with inhibitory activity serve as upstream regulators that suppress protein synthesis. By acting on the translational machinery, these inhibitory enzymes can reduce or halt the production of new proteins, a mechanism that is central to cellular control of growth, response to stress, and the action of certain antimicrobial agents. Understanding the hierarchical relationship in which inhibitory enzymů are the primary agents that give rise to inhibitory syntézy proteinů helps clarify how protein synthesis is modulated in both normal physiology and disease states.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1671
retrieved_docs.content: Spectinomycin is classified as an aminoglycoside antibacterial agent, indicating that it shares the pharmacologic properties typical of aminoglycoside antibiotics, such as inhibition of bacterial protein synthesis. Chemically, spectinomycin is derived from heterocyklické sloučeniny tricyklické, meaning that its molecular framework is based on a heterocyclic tricyclic parent structure. This dual classification highlights both its therapeutic role as a broad‑spectrum antibacterial drug and its underlying tricyclic heterocyclic chemistry, which together inform its mechanism of action, spectrum of activity, and considerations for clinical use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1484
retrieved_docs.content: Ganciclovir is an antiviral agent whose mechanism of action is classified as a nucleic acid synthesis inhibitor, meaning it interferes with the synthesis of viral DNA and thereby suppresses viral replication. This pharmacologic property places ganciclovir within the broader category of nucleic acid synthesis inhibitors, highlighting its role in targeting viral replication processes and informing its use in the treatment of infections such as cytomegalovirus.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 570
retrieved_docs.content: regulators of biosynthetic pathways. Recognizing these relationships clarifies how antimetabolite and immunosuppressive agents are organized, their mechanistic targets, biosynthetic requirements, and potential for resistance, providing essential insight for patient management, metabolic research, and therapeutic development.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 709
retrieved_docs.content: Deoxyribonucleases / Plasmin is identified as the active ingredient of the human fibrinolysin preparation, indicating its therapeutic use in fibrinolytic therapy. This enzyme is derived from, or closely related to, plasmin, reflecting its origin in the physiological fibrinolytic pathway. Classified within the category of hematological enzymes, Deoxyribonucleases / Plasmin belongs to the broader group “Enzymes, hematological,” which in turn is a subset of the larger enzyme family termed deoxyribonukleasy. Within this family, streptodornase represents a more narrowly defined enzyme, highlighting the hierarchical organization of these nucleic‑acid‑degrading proteins. Understanding these relationships clarifies how Deoxyribonucleases / Plasmin fits into both clinical applications and the broader taxonomy of hematological and deoxyribonuclease enzymes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1694
retrieved_docs.content: Deoxyribonukleasy is classified as a member of the hematological enzyme group, indicating its role among enzymes that function in blood‑related processes. Within this broader category, streptodornase represents a more specific subclass of deoxyribonukleasy, reflecting a narrower relationship that denotes streptodornase as a particular type of this hematological enzyme.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2027
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin B12, encompassing cyanocobalamin and its analogues, includes several distinct cobalamin forms such as mecobalamin and hydroxokobalamin, which are recognized as member compounds within this vitamin family.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 830
retrieved_docs.content: Cyclic amines, direct acting antivirals are classified as direct acting antivirals, indicating that this group of cyclic amine compounds belongs to the broader class of antiviral agents that directly inhibit viral replication by targeting specific viral enzymes or proteins.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1374
retrieved_docs.content: Aminoglycoside antibacterials are a class of antibiotics whose primary mechanism of action is the inhibition of bacterial protein synthesis. Micronomicin is a specific agent that belongs to this aminoglycoside class, exemplifying the typical properties of these drugs. Although the term “daptomycin” appears alongside aminoglycoside antibacterials, daptomycin is not an aminoglycoside; it is a separate lipopeptide antibiotic with a different mechanism of action. The relationship described as “inverse_isa” reflects the functional link that aminoglycoside antibacterials exert inhibitory effects on protein synthesis, which is a defining characteristic of this drug class.
